title: The Witness State in Kundalini: When You Watch Yourself From Outside

body:
The Witness State in Kundalini: When You Watch Yourself From Outside

Hey. You're probably reading this because you're scared. I get it. It's 3 AM, your heart's racing, and you just had another one of those experiences. The ones where it feels like you're not really you, not really in your body. Like you're watching a movie of your own life, but you're the main character and the audience all at once. You're wondering if you're losing your mind. I've been there. I know that feeling of cold dread settling in your stomach, that desperate search for an explanation, for someone - anyone - who understands what you're going through. This isn't some abstract spiritual concept for you right now, is it? This is your reality, and it's terrifying.

What you're experiencing, what many of us in the kundalini community call the "witness state" or "observer consciousness," it's real. It's not a hallucination, not psychosis in the way a doctor might diagnose it. Not always, anyway. It's a fundamental shift in perception that can happen when your energy system starts reorganizing itself, when kundalini wakes up and starts doing its thing. It's like your consciousness detaches itself from the usual grip of your ego, your personality, your body, and suddenly you're seeing everything from a distance. Even yourself. Right?

It's disorienting as hell. You feel disconnected, estranged, like you're a puppet and someone else is pulling the strings. You might find yourself doing things, saying things, reacting to things, and there's this part of you that's just watching, utterly detached, sometimes even with a weird sense of amusement or horror. It's not you, but it is you. It's a paradox that can drive you absolutely crazy if you don't have a context for it. And let's be honest, most of us don't. We're not taught this stuff in school. We're not prepared for our inner world to shatter and rearrange itself like this.

I remember the first time it really hit me hard. I was in a conversation, a perfectly normal, mundane conversation, and suddenly, it was like I was floating above my own head, watching myself talk. I could see my gestures, hear the tone of my voice, even predict what I was going to say next, all while still saying it. It was like a split screen, two realities playing out simultaneously. My body was on autopilot, and my true self, my consciousness, was just an observer. I wanted to scream, to stop the conversation, to shake myself and say, "What the hell is going on?!" But I couldn't. I just kept going, smiling, nodding, all while this silent, internal panic was unfolding. Does that land? Does that sound familiar?

This isn't just some intellectual idea. This is a visceral, often frightening, experience. It can make you question your sanity, your identity, everything you thought you knew about yourself. And you're not alone in that. So many of us have walked this path, feeling like we were falling apart, only to realize later that we were actually coming together in a much bigger, more expansive way.

The Mechanics of Detachment: Why It Happens

So, why does this happen? Why does your consciousness decide to take a step back and watch the show? Look, there isn't one single, universally accepted answer for kundalini phenomena, especially not in mainstream science. But from the perspective of those who've been through it, and from ancient spiritual traditions, it's often seen as a necessary part of the process. Think of it like this: your ego, your small "self," is a construct. It's built up over years of experiences, beliefs, traumas, and conditioning. It's how you deal with the world, how you define yourself. But it's not the whole truth of who you are.

When kundalini awakens, it's like a powerful spiritual current starts flowing through your system. This energy wants to purify, to clear out all the old junk, all the blockages, all the false beliefs that are keeping you from realizing your true nature. And one of the biggest blockages is the ego's identification with everything - your thoughts, your emotions, your body, your story. The witness state is, in a way, kundalini's mechanism for loosening that grip. It's forcing you to see that you are not your thoughts, you are not your emotions, you are not even your body. You are the awareness that perceives all of those things.

It's not always gentle. Sometimes it feels like a violent uprooting. You might feel a deep sense of depersonalization or derealization. The world might seem flat, unreal, like a stage play. Your own actions might feel robotic or automatic. This isn't because you're losing touch with reality; it's because your perception of reality is shifting. You're starting to see the illusion, the constructed nature of what you always took for granted as "real." And that can be incredibly destabilizing.

The Purpose of the Observer

But here's the thing: this detachment, as scary as it feels, has a purpose. It's giving you space. Space to observe your patterns, your reactions, your unconscious programming, without being completely swept away by them. Before, when you got angry, you were anger. Now, you might still feel anger, but there's a part of you that's watching it, noticing it, recognizing it as a temporary state, not your entire being. This is where real transformation can begin.

It's like being able to finally see the matrix, you know? To realize that a lot of what you thought was solid and fixed about your identity is actually just programming. And once you see it, you can start to question it, to change it, or simply to let it go. This process is messy. It's not neat and tidy. You'll have moments of clarity followed by moments of intense confusion. You'll feel like you're making progress, then feel like you're regressing completely. That's normal. That's how it goes.

I've seen this happen countless times, both in myself and in others. The initial shock gives way to a deeper understanding, sometimes grudgingly. You start to realize that this observer isn't something external trying to mess with you; it's an aspect of your own consciousness that's always been there, just hidden by the noise of the ego. It's your true self, peeking through the cracks, trying to show you something important. It's trying to show you that you are bigger than your problems, bigger than your personality, bigger than anything you could have imagined.

Coping with the Disconnect: Grounding Techniques

Okay, so you're in this state. What do you do? Because knowing why it's happening doesn't always stop the terror. The key is grounding, and I can't stress this enough. When you feel like you're floating away, you need to anchor yourself back into your body, into the present moment. This isn't about shutting down the witness state - you probably can't anyway - it's about finding stability within it. It's about creating a safe container for this intense experience.

Here are some things that have helped me and others:

  • Sensory Focus: Get really granular with your senses. What do you see right now? Not just "a room," but the specific color of the wall, the texture of the fabric on your chair, the dust motes dancing in the light. What do you hear? The hum of the refrigerator, your own breathing, the distant traffic. What do you feel? The clothes on your skin, the temperature of the air, the pressure of your feet on the floor. Engage all five senses, one by one, with intense focus. This pulls your awareness out of the detached head-space and back into the physical.
  • Physical Movement: Get your body moving. Go for a walk. Do some stretching. Yoga, even gentle movements, can be incredibly helpful. The goal isn't to distract yourself, but to consciously inhabit your body. Feel your muscles working, feel your breath. Dance, if you feel like it. Just move.
  • Nature Connection: Spend time outdoors. Put your bare feet on the earth. Feel the sun on your skin, the wind in your hair. Touch a tree. Listen to the birds. Nature has a way of naturally bringing us back into balance, reminding us of our physical connection to the world.
  • Mindful Eating/Drinking: Make your next meal or drink an exercise in mindfulness. Don't just shovel food in. Pay attention to the smell, the taste, the texture, how it feels in your mouth, how your body reacts to it. Take slow sips of water, feeling it go down your throat. This is a powerful way to bring your awareness back to the present and to your physical self.
  • Journaling: Write down everything you're experiencing, no filter. Don't worry about grammar or making sense. Just get it all out. Sometimes seeing your thoughts on paper can help you gain a little distance from them, even within the witness state. It's like you're processing it externally, which can be less overwhelming than keeping it all inside.

Remember, you're not trying to stop the process, you're trying to integrate it. You're learning to live with this expanded awareness, to find your footing in a new reality. It takes time. It takes patience. And it takes a lot of self-compassion. Don't beat yourself up for feeling scared or confused. It's a natural reaction to something so utterly outside of normal human experience.

Seeking Support: You Don't Have to Do This Alone

This is where I tell you, honestly, you cannot do this alone. Or rather, you shouldn't. Trying to white-knuckle your way through a kundalini awakening, especially when you're experiencing intense witness states, is a recipe for prolonged suffering. You need support. You need people who understand, or at least who are willing to listen without judgment.

Here's what I recommend, because I've seen it work:

  • Find a Kundalini-Aware Therapist/Coach: This is crucial. Not just any therapist will do. You need someone who understands spiritual emergencies, who won't immediately pathologize your experiences. Someone who knows that what you're going through, while intense, isn't necessarily a sign of mental illness, but rather a spiritual process. Look for terms like "transpersonal psychology," "spiritual emergence," or "kundalini integration." There are people out there who get it. I can't stress this enough.
  • Connect with Others: Online forums, support groups - find your tribe. Knowing you're not the only one going through this is a huge relief. Sharing your experiences, listening to others, it normalizes what feels utterly bizarre. It reminds you that you're part of something bigger, not just an isolated case of madness.
  • Educate Yourself: Read books about kundalini and spiritual emergency. The more you understand the process, the less terrifying it becomes. Knowledge is power, especially when you're facing something so unknown. I'd recommend starting with books like Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link). It's a classic for a reason. Also, The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) is a good one, it provides a lot of firsthand accounts. Bonnie Greenwell has also written some really helpful books, like When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) and The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link). These can provide invaluable context and reassurance.
  • Be Honest with Loved Ones (Carefully): This one's tricky. You might not want to tell everyone, because most people won't understand. But if you have one or two trusted friends or family members who you think might be open-minded, try to explain what you're going through. Even if they don't fully get it, just having someone know, someone you can talk to, can make a huge difference.

Don't isolate yourself. That's the worst thing you can do when you're feeling disconnected already. Reach out. Even if it's just an anonymous online forum at 3 AM. Someone's listening. Someone's been there. We're here.

The Long Game: Integration, Not Eradication

Here's the truth: the witness state, for many, isn't something that just goes away. It becomes a permanent part of your experience. But it changes. The raw terror eventually subsides. The intense disorientation mellows out. What once felt like a symptom of madness starts to feel like a powerful tool, a gift. You learn to live with it, to appreciate the perspective it offers.

You'll find that this observer consciousness gives you an incredible capacity for self-awareness. You'll see your own emotional triggers before they completely hijack you. You'll notice your habitual thought patterns and have the choice to engage with them or let them pass. It gives you a sense of inner freedom you never had before. You're no longer completely at the mercy of your internal world. You have an anchor, a steady point of awareness that can observe the storms without being capsized by them.

It's like learning to drive a new, incredibly powerful car. At first, you're overwhelmed, you might crash a few times. But with practice, with guidance, you learn to handle it. You learn to appreciate its power, to use it skillfully. This isn't about getting rid of the witness state; it's about integrating it into your daily life, making it a conscious, helpful ally rather than a terrifying, unbidden guest.

So yeah, you're not crazy. You're undergoing a deep transformation. It's messy, it's scary, and it's probably the hardest thing you'll ever go through. But you're not alone. Keep breathing. Keep grounding. Keep seeking support. This too shall pass - not the state itself, perhaps, but the fear and confusion that come with it. You've got this. One breath at a time.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
